text: Abstract: As pre-trained language models (PLMs) and the pre-training–fine-tuning framework have become prevalent in the natural language processing community, it has been observed that larger models usually lead to better performance. However, the fine-tuning and storage of large models with all their parameters is often computationally expensive and practically infeasible, which calls for a new research direction of parameter-efficient adaptation of PLMs. Typically, parameter-efficient adaptations aim to optimize a small subset of model parameters and keep the majority of the original parameters in PLMs unchanged, so that the computation and storage costs are greatly reduced. We found out that in general, large-scale models can be effectively triggered by adjusting a small number of parameters. Then, no matter how different the parameter-efficient adaptations are implemented, we name them as delta-tuning. The term “delta” is a notation conventionally used to represent the difference or change. We bring this notation into parameter adjustment in the models and call the parameters to be trained as the “delta”. In this paper, we formally define the definition of the delta-tuning, and propose a uniform criterial for the current existing parameter-efficient adaptation implementations to understand their connections and differences. We then explain how the delta-tuning works from the aspect of optimization and optimal control theories. Finally, a series of experiments are conducted on over 100 NLP tasks to demonstrate the overall performance and characteristics of the parameter-efficient adaptation methods for PLMs.
